mnemonics: AWT: The Ancestor With Tools. Swing: Stylish Son With Improvements. Remember that Swing is the stylish son built on the foundation of its ancestor (AWT).
keyPoints: Swing components are built on top of AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) classes and use AWT's event handling and painting model.
Swing provides a richer set of lightweight, platform-independent components (like JFrame, JButton) compared to AWT's heavyweight, peer-dependent components.
While Swing uses AWT's foundation, it paints its own components using Java code rather than relying on the native operating system's windowing toolkit.
commonMistakes: Assuming Swing and AWT are completely separate and independent toolkits, rather than understanding Swing's dependency on AWT.
Using AWT components (Frame, Button) and Swing components (JFrame, JButton) interchangeably or mixing them incorrectly within the same container, which can cause rendering issues.
Believing that Swing components are heavyweight like AWT components, when they are actually lightweight and painted directly by the Java runtime.
